title: Kryptothera - The Cryptid Pursuit (Overview)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hJ3DwYLFjcY

Daniel (@dungeondive) gives an overview of Kryptothera: The Cryptid Pursuit from Knock to Lux Games, working from a publisher review copy — he deliberately didn't back the Kickstarter, and he's upfront about why. At its core this is a competitive game: up to six guilds of cryptid hunters range across a North American map, tracking and capturing cryptids, then hauling them back to headquarters to convert into coins and victory points over ten rounds. The competitive focus, a daunting eight expansions offered during the campaign, and a table footprint too large for his small desk all kept him away.

Playing two-handed solo, he found plenty to admire. The event deck is his favourite element — gorgeous art, sharp flavour text, table-shaking twists like everyone passing their cryptid hand to the left. He praises the art-deco look, the near-total absence of plastic (lovely wood standees and tokens), the wallet-versus-income money management, the contracts, and a genuine push-your-luck tension over when to retreat before rivals steal your caged catch (PvP opens at round seven; legendaries flood the board at round ten).

His chief gripe: the ~90 cryptids are little more than art plus a stat check, with no choice of what to do with one once caught. There's an official solo mode, "the Descent," he doesn't have — but he's more curious now than before.

----

When a game is competitive at its core but you mostly play solo, does a "take that" PvP layer like Kryptothera's enhance the two-handed experience or just get in the way?
